title: Webhooks, Replay Defense, SSRF, Abuse, and Sensitive Business Flows · Guided Systems Studio
summary: A server pairing session predicts request, handler, dependency, response, and cleanup transitions before building the first webhook authenticity, replay, and abuse defense for webhooks, replay defense, ssrf, abuse, and sensitive business flows. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of webhook authenticity, replay, and abuse defense while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidenc…
objectives: Bound and retain exact raw body bytes, select expected signature scheme and key, compare in constant time, and only then parse and authorize the event.
Validate timestamp or nonce window, event ID uniqueness, tenant, type, ordering, duplicate delivery, and durable processing state before effects.
Acknowledge only after durable acceptance, move long work to bounded processing, classify retries, make effects idempotent, and expose operator recovery.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Webhook Raw Signature
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop team handles case 6692 in a public status API serving assistive and low-bandwidth clients. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine raw-message webhook authentication, replay bounds, and SSRF egress policy; then repeat the request with a different method and retain status plus durable-effect evidence. Browser code models decisions only. Listener, TLS, database, proxy, race, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Webhook Raw Signature.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for bound and retain exact raw body bytes, select expected signature scheme and key, compare in constant time, and only then parse and authorize the event. For Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Webhook Raw Signature, keep Webhook Raw Signature connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Business Flow Abuse happy path, omit method, authority, credential, byte, time, redirect, retry, and cleanup boundaries, then accept plausible output. This does not satisfy Business Flow Abuse for webhook authenticity, replay, and abuse defense.

steps.title: Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Business Flow Abuse
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Business Flow Abuse: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop team handles case 6701 in a multi-tenant case service with a bounded PostgreSQL pool. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine raw-message webhook authentication, replay bounds, and SSRF egress policy; then lower the byte or time budget and prove rejection occurs at the owning boundary. Browser code models decisions only. Listener, TLS, database, proxy, race, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Bound costly domain actions by principal, resource, workflow state, velocity, inventory, and anomaly evidence beyond raw request rate.

Evidence target: Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Business Flow Abuse.
steps.why: Retrieving Business Flow Abuse through the concrete webhook authenticity, replay, and abuse defense case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Business Flow Abuse, keep Business Flow Abuse connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Bound costly domain actions by principal, resource, workflow state, velocity, inventory, and anomaly evidence beyond raw request rate.

hints: For Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Business Flow Abuse, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Business Flow Abuse precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Business Flow Abuse, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A workshop team handles case 6701 in a multi-tenant case service with a bounded PostgreSQL pool. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine raw-message webhook authentication, replay bounds, and SSRF egress policy; then lower the byte or time budget and prove rejection occurs at the owning boundary. Browser code models decisions only. Listener, TLS, database, proxy, race, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Bound costly domain actions by principal, resource, workflow state, velocity, inventory, and anomaly evidence beyond raw request rate.

For Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Business Flow Abuse, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: A generic requests-per-minute limit prevents scraping, scalping, spam, and workflow automation abuse.
